retrieved_docs.title: Small Cell Lung Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Extensive Stage
retrieved_docs.content: Novel agents for SCLC are currently under investigation and include immunotherapies and targeted therapies previously approved for NSCLC and other malignancies. Immunotherapies include immune checkpoint inhibitors Nivolumab (PD-1 inhibitor antibody), Pembrolizumab (anti-PD1 antibody), Ipilimumab (CTLA-4 inhibitor antibody). Targeted therapies in clinical trials include Rovalpituzumab tesirine (Delta-like protein three antibody-drug conjugate). [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor–Associated Acute Kidney Injury -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs), which target the cytotoxic T-lymphocyte–associated antigen 4 (CTLA-4), programmed death-1 (PD-1), programmed death–ligand-1 (PD-L1), and lymphocyte activation gene-3 ( LAG-3 ) pathways, have revolutionized cancer treatment. In 2011, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved ipilimumab (the first ICI) for the treatment of advanced melanoma. Since then, the number of approved ICIs and their indications have increased exponentially, demonstrating the extraordinary potential of these drugs in treating various tumors. Currently, 11 ICIs are approved for nearly 20 types of cancer, including melanoma, lung cancer, renal cell carcinoma, and endometrial cancer, with indications continuing to expand over time. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Non–Small Cell Lung Cancer -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: A variety of other mutations, including ROS1, BRAF, RET, TRK, MET, KRAS, can also be found, and for these, their specific inhibitors should be integrated into the treatment plan. If a driver mutation is absent or unknown, the level of programmed cell death ligand 1 (PD-L1) expression should be quantified, and if greater than 50 percent, then pembrolizumab or atezolizumab may be utilized in the treatment plan. These can also be used as single-agent treatment without chemotherapy. [17] Lastly, patients should be actively assessed for eligibility for clinical trials during their disease course.

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Immunotherapy for NSCLC
retrieved_docs.content: Immunotherapy boosts the immune system and helps it recognize cancer cells as foreign, and increases their responsiveness. There are several check-points to decrease autoimmunity and autodestruction of the body’s cells by the immune system. Malignant cells co-opt these check-points and create tolerance in the immune system. Of these check-points, programmed-death receptor 1 (PD-1) has been of particular interest recently. PD-1 plays an important role in down-regulating T-cells and promotes self-tolerance. However, it also renders the immune system less effective against tumor cells. PD-1 interacts with two proteins: PD-L1 and PD-L2. This binding results in the inactivation of activated T-cells. At the moment, there are antibodies approved for PD-1 and its ligand, PD-L1 only. They inhibit the PD-1 receptor directly or bind to PD-L1, thus preventing it from inactivating the activated T-cell.

retrieved_docs.title: Nivolumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Non–small cell lung cancer Metastatic, with progression during or after platinum-based chemotherapy (and after progression under targeted therapies for EGFR or ALK genomic tumor aberrations in case these aberrations are present). [2] As first-line therapy combined with ipilimumab in metastatic or recurrent non–small cell lung cancer with a PD-L1 expression equal or higher to 1% with no EGFR or ALK genomic tumor aberrations. [3] As first-line therapy in combination with ipilimumab, given with 2 cycles of platinum-doublet chemotherapy. Renal cell carcinoma After prior anti­angiogenic therapy. Previously untreated, of intermediate or poor risk, in combination with ipilimumab. [4] Squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck Recurrent or metastatic with progressive disease on or following a platinum-based therapy. [5] Pleural Mesothelioma In combination with ipilimumab for unresectable pleural mesothelioma. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Immunotherapy -- Mechanism of Action -- Several Immunotherapy Modalities Used in Cancer Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Antigen Recognition: The extracellular domain of the CAR is engineered to recognize a specific antigen present on the surface of cancer cells. This antigen is often a tumor-associated antigen (TAA) or a cancer-specific antigen.

retrieved_docs.title: Atezolizumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Atezolizumab monotherapy is indicated for patients with metastatic NSCLC with high PD-L1 expression, regardless of histologic type. These patients are confirmed by an FDA-approved test and have no EGFR or ALK genomic tumor aberrations. PD-L1 stained ≥50% of tumor cells (TC ≥50%) PD-L1 stained tumor-infiltrating immune cells (IC) covering ≥10% of the tumor area (IC ≥10%)

retrieved_docs.title: Pembrolizumab -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: In NSCLC, for example, Brody et al. conducted a large meta-analysis of thirty-five different clinical trials and showed many studies that linked higher PD-L1 expression with worse survival, but also several other studies that showed no such effect. [33] Additionally, PD-L1 as a predictive biomarker for checkpoint inhibitor treatment effect showed better success in higher expressers in some studies, but also no association in several other studies. [33] Consequently, although response and survival can correlate with PD-L1 expression, alternative methods are needed to stratify which specific patients are most likely to benefit. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Atezolizumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Atezolizumab is a humanized IgG1 monoclonal antibody that targets programmed cell death ligand 1 (PD-L1). Atezolizumab has received FDA approval for multiple neoplastic conditions and may be administered as monotherapy or in combination with chemotherapy. Indications include locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma, metastatic non-small cell lung cancer, extensive-stage small cell lung cancer, metastatic triple-negative breast cancer, and unresectable or metastatic hepatocellular carcinoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Immunotherapy -- Mechanism of Action -- Several Immunotherapy Modalities Used in Cancer Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Challenges and Side Effects: Despite its efficacy, CAR T-cell therapy can be associated with side effects such as cytokine release syndrome (CRS) and neurotoxicity. Managing these side effects and improving the therapy's safety profile are ongoing research areas.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy for Early-Stage Non–Small Cell Lung Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Combination Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: SBRT and surgical resection combination in stage I (T1/2N0) patients has been investigated in small phase 2 trials. The Measuring the Integration of Stereotactic Ablative Radiotherapy Plus Surgery for Early-Stage Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (MISSILE) trial combined SBRT followed by VATS lobectomy or sublobar resection after 10 weeks with a 2-year overall survival of 77% and local control rates of 100%. The pathologic complete response rate was lower than expected at 60%. [21] The introduction of immunotherapy has led to marked improvements in oncologic outcomes for patients with NSCLC. However, patients diagnosed with early-stage disease are typically not offered systemic therapy as definitive local therapy with surgery or radiation can be potentially curative. A phase 2 trial investigated the role of durvalumab combined with SBRT and surgical resection. The pathologic complete response was 27% versus 0% without immunotherapy. The low rate of pCR in the local therapy alone arm may have been due to the low doses of radiation (biologically equivalent dose 43 Gy) and the short time interval between SBRT and surgery. [22]

retrieved_docs.content: Malignant neoplasms of the bronchus are anatomically associated with the thoracic cavity, underscoring the clinical relevance of thoracic anatomy in oncologic assessment. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1885
retrieved_docs.content: Bronchoscopy through tracheostomy with biopsy of lung is a diagnostic procedure that directly targets the plíce (lung) as its procedural site. Imaging of the chest with computed tomography that demonstrates a miliary pulmonary infiltrate also identifies the plíce as the anatomical location of these diffuse infiltrative findings. In addition, the plíce serves as the primary organ from which سرطان الغدة الرئوية (pulmonary gland carcinoma) can arise, highlighting the lung’s role both as a site for invasive diagnostic interventions and as the origin of specific malignant disease. Understanding these interconnections is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate procedural planning, and effective management of lung‑related pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2349
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on lung and bronchus are classified as a specific subset within the broader category of other diagnostic procedures on lung and bronchus, indicating that these lung‑ and bronchus‑focused diagnostic interventions fall under the larger group of related diagnostic procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1563
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Lung Hilum Neoplasm is essentially the same entity as cancer of bronchus; lung, indicating that it represents a malignant tumor arising in the hilum of the lung. Both terms are linked to the anatomic site “plíce,” the lung, establishing the primary location of the disease. In the classification hierarchy, cancer of bronchus; lung falls under the broader category of سرطان الشعب الهوائية (bronchial cancer), confirming that this malignancy is a subtype of bronchial carcinoma. Radiologic evaluation such as computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate commonly identifies findings within the plíce, reflecting the typical pulmonary involvement of this disease. The lung (plíce) is also associated with other related neoplasms, including the child entity سرطان الغدة الرئوية (pulmonary gland tumor), underscoring the spectrum of lung‑related cancers. Together, these relationships clarify that Malignant Lung Hilum Neoplasm/cancer of bronchus; lung is a bronchial carcinoma situated in the lung, detectable by CT imaging, and part of a broader group of pulmonary malignancies that includes specific subtypes like pulmonary gland tumor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1820
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography of the chest that demonstrates a miliary pulmonary infiltrate represents a radiologic finding situated within the lungs (plíce). The lungs, as an organ system, serve as the anatomical source for lung gland carcinoma (سرطان الغدة الرئوية). Recognizing that miliary infiltrates are identified in the pulmonary tissue underscores the importance of evaluating such imaging patterns for potential underlying malignant processes, including lung gland carcinoma, thereby informing diagnostic assessment and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1933
retrieved_docs.content: CT angiography of thorax with contrast is classified as a chest CT scan. The diagnostic thoracic computed tomography performed without contrast material also falls under the category of chest CT scan, representing a more specific subcategory within chest CT imaging. Additionally, a computed tomography of the chest that identifies a mass of the superior mediastinum utilizes chest CT scanning as the method for detecting such findings. These relationships illustrate how various CT techniques—contrast‑enhanced angiography, non‑contrast diagnostic scans, and targeted imaging for mediastinal masses—are all encompassed by the broader concept of a chest CT scan, highlighting their roles in diagnostic imaging and patient evaluation.

Disease entities mapped to the respiratory anatomy include malignant lung hilum neoplasm, cancer of the bronchus (سرطان الشعب الهوائية), and malignant bronchial neoplasm. Imaging findings such as miliary pulmonary infiltrates on chest computed tomography support diagnostic correlation with bronchial carcinoma. Together, these standardized vocabularies integrate anatomical structures, laboratory measurements, chemicals, procedural modalities, disease categories, imaging findings, and social factors across multiple languages, providing a comprehensive framework for consistent data capture, analysis, and multidisciplinary patient care in clinical research and practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 803
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of the head and neck is a specific subtype of squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck and is morphologically associated with metastatic squamous cell carcinoma (سرطانة الخلايا الحرشفية النقيلية). Squamous cell carcinoma of the trachea is classified within the “cancer; other respiratory and intrathoracic” category, which in turn is part of the broader non‑hematologic malignant diseases SMQ (الأمراض غير الدموية الخبيثة (SMQ)). The non‑hematologic malignant diseases SMQ encompasses a range of distinct malignancies, including adenocarcinoma of the prostate (سرطان البروستاتا الغدي), squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck (سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق), lung carcinoma of non‑smokers (سرطان الرئة للخلايا عديمة الرائحة), and metastatic squamous cell carcinoma (سرطانة الخلايا الحرشفية النقيلية). Together, these relationships illustrate how specific head‑and‑neck and respiratory cancers are organized within larger diagnostic categories, aiding clinicians in classification, diagnosis, and management of malignant diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2159
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography of the chest revealing a miliary pulmonary infiltrate is a radiologic finding that occurs in the lungs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 886
retrieved_docs.content: Squamous cell carcinoma of the trachea is categorized as a cancer of the respiratory and intrathoracic system. Patients with this malignancy may present with سُعالٌ (cough) as a clinical finding, making cough a potential indicator of tracheal squamous cell carcinoma and underscoring its relevance for early diagnosis and appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2419
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnostic protocol termed “Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material, followed by contrast material(s) and further sections” represents a more specific form of a chest CT scan, indicating that a non‑contrast thoracic CT is performed first and then followed by contrast‑enhanced imaging with additional sections. This refined chest CT technique is employed to evaluate thoracic pathology and serves as a method for detecting distinct radiographic findings. In particular, chest CT imaging can reveal a mass located in the mid mediastinum, as described by “computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum,” and can also identify a pattern of miliary pulmonary infiltrates, referred to as “computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate.” Recognizing these specific findings on a detailed chest CT study aids clinicians in diagnosing mediastinal lesions and diffuse pulmonary disease, thereby informing appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1303
retrieved_docs.content: Bronchoscopy with drainage of lung abscess is a therapeutic procedure that is performed with an indirect procedural site in the plíce (lung). Malignant Lung Hilum Neoplasm is a malignant disease that has the plíce as its associated anatomic site, indicating that the tumor originates in or involves the lung hilum. The imaging study described as computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate identifies a radiologic finding located in the plíce, reflecting diffuse tiny nodular infiltrates within the lung parenchyma. In addition, the plíce is hierarchically linked to سرطان الغدة الرئوية, a specific type of lung gland carcinoma, as a child entity of the lung. Together, these relationships underscore the central role of the plíce in interventional bronchoscopy, malignant lung pathology, radiologic assessment, and specific lung cancer subtypes, all of which are critical considerations for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.
